turns.content: So I've been following this talk on crossbeam and it mentions a skip list as the underlying data structure, which sounds like the same thing I saw in C++ STL but somehow this Rust version feels different. It seems to be built around the `crossbeam-skiplist` crate instead of standard library collections.

That's exactly right and it is a great comparison point because while you might have seen skip lists in other languages, this one is specifically designed for concurrency in Rust using the crossbeam ecosystem. The key difference here is that it doesn't rely on traditional locks like `Arc<Mutex<T>>` or `Rc<RefCell<T>>` which are common in standard library collections but introduce contention and performance bottlenecks when many threads access the data simultaneously.
I see, so instead of using those mutexes we get something lock-free? How does that actually work under the hood?

You hit the nail on the head regarding that distinction because a lock-free skip list avoids holding locks entirely by leveraging atomic operations and memory ordering guarantees provided by the Rust standard library traits like `AtomicUsize` or `AtomicPointer`. The implementation in crossbeam-skiplist uses these primitives to perform comparisons and updates without needing any external synchronization mechanisms, which means multiple threads can read and write concurrently without blocking each other.
That sounds efficient but what about the ordering guarantees? Can I still rely on the list being sorted even with all this chaos?

Yes you absolutely can because the algorithm maintains strict invariants through careful use of compare-and-swap operations and memory barriers that ensure visibility across threads. The crossbeam-skiplist implementation guarantees that every read operation sees a consistent snapshot of the data structure at some point in time while write operations are ordered correctly so the sorted property is preserved despite concurrent modifications.
So if I'm trying to implement my own version using the `crossbeam` crate, do I need to handle memory ordering manually or does it abstract that away?

The crossbeam-skiplist crate handles most of this abstraction for you by wrapping the low-level atomic operations in a high-level API so you don't have to manually reason about memory ordering every time. However understanding how `AtomicUsize` and `AtomicPointer` work under the hood is still valuable because it helps you debug performance issues or optimize critical paths if the built-in version isn't quite fitting your specific concurrency needs.
Got it, but I'm curious about the actual traversal logic. How do we jump through levels efficiently when multiple threads are updating nodes?

The traversal works by following pointers stored in each node which allows skipping over large chunks of data rather than walking every single element sequentially. When a thread updates a node it uses atomic operations to update its pointer fields directly without acquiring locks, so other threads can continue their traversals based on the latest visible state of those pointers without needing to wait for completion of all writes.

To clarify your question about the implementation details: are you currently building a custom skip list or just evaluating whether to adopt crossbeam-skiplist for an existing project? This helps me tailor my explanation toward either implementing from scratch or integrating into your current codebase.

I'm leaning towards adoption since I already have some data structures in place and don't want to reinvent the wheel. But I want to make sure it integrates well with other crossbeam primitives like channels.

That's a solid approach because crossbeam-skiplist plays nicely with other primitives like `crossbeam_channel` or `crossbeam_queue` since they all share the same philosophy of zero-copy operations and lock-free design. You can safely combine them in your programs without worrying about deadlock scenarios that often plague traditional concurrent programming patterns.
One last thing before I start coding: does this support range queries efficiently like a balanced tree would?

Yes it does because the skip list structure inherently supports efficient range searches by allowing you to jump directly to the lower bound of your query range and then iterate forward through the linked nodes until you reach the upper bound. This makes operations like finding all elements within a specific interval significantly faster than linear scans over entire collections.

I've read about the crossbeam-skiplist crate and how it uses a skip list for ordered concurrent access, but I still don't fully understand why the lock-free approach actually improves performance in multi-threaded scenarios compared to standard mutexes.

The key difference lies in how threads acquire resources. With a standard Mutex, one thread holds the lock until explicitly released, blocking others entirely during critical sections like updating or reading the skip list nodes. In contrast, the crossbeam-skiplist relies on atomic operations and compare-and-swap logic to allow multiple threads to access different parts of the data structure simultaneously without any central coordinator holding a shared lock.
That makes sense regarding blocking, but I'm confused about the memory ordering guarantees. How does the skip list ensure visibility across cores when using these atomic operations?

The crossbeam-skiplist crate specifically uses Rust's `std::sync::atomic` primitives with full memory ordering semantics to guarantee that updates made by one thread become immediately visible to others without relying on a global lock. Each node in the skip list is protected by appropriate atomic flags and pointers, ensuring that once Thread A writes a value to its local cache or modifies a pointer, those changes are published to all other cores through the memory model defined by these atomics.
I've read about skip lists before, but I'm still confused on how to actually implement them in Rust without deadlocks. Can you help me understand the basics?

You are right that standard skip lists rely heavily on locks for safety, which is exactly what makes them unsuitable for high-concurrency scenarios. The real magic comes from using a lock-free approach where threads only access nodes they own through atomic operations like compare-and-swap instead of acquiring the same mutex multiple times.
So you're telling me that crossbeam's skip list implementation doesn't use any locks at all? That seems too good to be true for a data structure this complex.

That is precisely the point of its design. The entire crate avoids using Rust's std::sync::Mutex because that would serialize access and defeat the purpose, instead relying on atomic operations to manage node ownership and transitions between levels.
I think I understand now, but let me double check. Are you saying the crossbeam skip list uses atomics directly without any intermediate locking?
Correct. It relies on atomic operations to manage node transitions between levels and ownership transfers so that concurrent readers and writers can coexist safely without ever needing a global lock.
That sounds efficient, but how do I actually integrate this into my existing project? Do I need to rewrite everything?

You can port your data structure by replacing the current lock-protected map with a crossbeam skip list. Your application logic remains mostly unchanged since you only swap out the underlying storage mechanism for something that offers similar read/write performance without blocking other threads.
Wait, I thought skip lists were just simpler versions of balanced trees? Why would anyone bother with this specific implementation instead of using a standard tree?

The key difference is that while balanced trees like red-black trees provide better worst-case bounds, they still require some form of synchronization to maintain their invariants under concurrent access. Skip lists achieve O(log n) expected performance without needing complex rebalancing logic or locks.
But what if two threads try to insert the same key at the same time? Won't that break everything?

That is exactly why you need a compare-and-swap operation. When inserting, the algorithm checks if the node already exists before attempting to add it, using atomics to prevent race conditions without requiring explicit locking.
So I should use crossbeam's skip list crate instead of writing my own implementation from scratch?
Yes. The crossbeam skip list crate provides a ready-to-use implementation that handles all the complexity of atomic operations and ownership management for you.
I'm still not sure how to handle deletion though, since I need to remove items occasionally. Is that supported?
Yes, it supports deletion by locating the target node and using atomic operations to transition it out of the structure while handling ownership correctly.
But what about searching? Does that require any special care with concurrency?
Searching is fully lock-free since readers only traverse nodes they can safely inspect without needing to modify anything or acquire locks.
Okay, so I have a clear picture now. Just replace my current map with this crossbeam skip list and everything will work smoothly?
Exactly. You simply swap out the storage layer while keeping your application logic intact.

Wait, I just realized something important about how this works. Since it uses atomics directly without locks, does that mean it can actually scale better than my current implementation with multiple cores?
Yes, because there is no contention on a shared mutex, threads don't have to wait for each other when accessing the data structure.
That's it? So I just need to make sure my code uses the right atomic operations and avoid any manual locking?
Correct. The crate handles all the low-level details of atomics and ownership so you only need to ensure your logic respects the lock-free nature.
I'm reading about crossbeam-skiplist and I keep thinking it's just a fancy version of std::collections::HashMap but with less locking. Is that right?

Not quite, the core distinction is how they handle concurrency. A standard HashMap uses an internal lock or Arc<Mutex> to protect its state, which means threads block each other when updating. The crossbeam-skiplist avoids that by using atomic compare-and-swap operations on nodes, letting multiple threads interleave without blocking.
So I don't need a Mutex for the whole collection anymore? That sounds like it would break my Rust style of avoiding deadlocks.

Exactly, you remove the global lock entirely. Instead of holding a reference to the entire map, each thread operates on its own view or uses local copies that get merged later via an algorithm like double-ended queue merging. This is why you can implement a concurrent skip list without ever needing a single Mutex for the whole structure.
But if I'm not using locks, how does it stay ordered? What's actually happening on the nodes?

The ordering is preserved through careful node updates that use atomic CAS operations. When a thread wants to insert or delete, it calculates which level in the skip list needs updating and performs an atomic compare-and-swap on those specific nodes. The key insight is that you never hold the lock while traversing; you only acquire it briefly for the actual modification.
I see, so I can still use Rc<RefCell> somewhere but not on the hot path?

That's a common confusion point. You might use RefCell for internal bookkeeping or temporary state management in your own code logic, but you never wrap the entire concurrent skip list container itself in an Arc<Mutex>. The lock-free nature comes from replacing the Mutex with atomic operations on the linked list nodes.
Can I just drop my current HashMap and start using this instead of worrying about contention?

You can, but be aware that the crossbeam-skiplist implementation might not exist yet in std. You'd need to use a crate like crossbeam-skiplist or implement your own using the atomic primitives from std::sync::atomic and the linked list structures you already know.
